title: DuckDB Public Repo System Review Graph
one_line: DuckDB turns SQL and local data access into vectorized analytical execution inside an embedded database engine.
scope: A public-safe system map of the DuckDB open-source repository based on public source directories and documentation.
bigger_picture: This example shows how to map a database engine repo. The system is not just a command-line tool or a client library; it is a layered execution engine where SQL is parsed, bound, planned, optimized, executed in vectorized operators, connected to storage and transactions, and extended through extensions. A reviewer should understand the route from query text to result chunks and persisted state.

source_links.label: GitHub repository
source_links.notes: Primary public source used for repo identity and source paths.

source_links.label: DuckDB documentation
source_links.notes: Public docs for SQL, clients, extensions, and engine behavior.

source_links.label: DuckDB internals overview
source_links.notes: Public internals documentation for engine orientation.

schemas.name: SQLRequest
schemas.purpose: Represents incoming SQL and execution context before parsing and binding.

schemas.name: LogicalPlan
schemas.purpose: Represents a query after parsing and semantic binding.

schemas.name: DataChunk
schemas.purpose: Represents batches of data flowing through vectorized execution.

schemas.name: StorageTransaction
schemas.purpose: Represents storage and transaction state for reads and writes.

schemas.name: ExtensionSpec
schemas.purpose: Describes extension-provided functionality and loading boundaries.

artifacts.name: Parser Source
artifacts.purpose: Turns SQL text into parsed statements.

artifacts.name: Planner Source
artifacts.purpose: Binds and plans parsed SQL into logical operators.

artifacts.name: Optimizer Source
artifacts.purpose: Rewrites and improves logical plans before physical execution.

artifacts.name: Execution Source
artifacts.purpose: Executes physical operators and data pipelines.

artifacts.name: Storage Source
artifacts.purpose: Handles table storage, persistence, scans, and writes.

artifacts.name: Transaction Source
artifacts.purpose: Coordinates transaction lifecycle and commit boundaries.

artifacts.name: Extension Tree
artifacts.purpose: Hosts built-in and optional extension surfaces such as parquet, json, icu, tpch, and tpcds.

artifacts.name: Test Suite
artifacts.purpose: Protects SQL behavior, storage behavior, extensions, and compatibility.

decision_gates.name: Parse And Bind Gate
decision_gates.risk_boundary: Invalid SQL or missing catalog references should not reach execution.

rules.if: SQL parses and all names/types bind
syntax, type, or catalog binding fails
rewrite is unsafe or not applicable

decision_gates.name: Execution Gate
decision_gates.risk_boundary: Physical operators should respect transaction and memory boundaries.

rules.if: runtime error, resource issue, or invalid state

decision_gates.name: Storage Commit Gate
decision_gates.risk_boundary: Writes should commit atomically or roll back.

decision_gates.name: Extension Load Gate
decision_gates.risk_boundary: Extensions should load only when compatible and allowed by policy.

rules.if: extension is compatible and policy allows loading

systems.name: SQL Front Door
systems.purpose: Accepts SQL from clients and turns it into parsed and bound statements.
systems.truth_boundary: A parsed query is not executable until binding and planning succeed.
systems.ideal_target: Query errors are caught early with clear semantics.

systems.name: Planner And Optimizer
systems.purpose: Builds logical plans and rewrites them into better executable forms.
systems.truth_boundary: Optimization must not change query meaning.
systems.ideal_target: The cheapest safe plan is selected for execution.

systems.name: Vectorized Execution Engine
systems.purpose: Executes physical operator pipelines over data chunks.
systems.truth_boundary: Execution depends on valid plan, memory, transaction, and storage state.
systems.ideal_target: Analytical queries execute predictably and efficiently.

systems.name: Storage And Transaction Layer
systems.purpose: Persists data, scans tables, and coordinates transaction boundaries.
systems.lifecycle: scan/write request -> storage state -> commit or rollback
systems.truth_boundary: Storage behavior is valid only within transaction rules.
systems.ideal_target: Reads and writes remain consistent across local analytical workloads.

systems.name: Extension System
systems.purpose: Adds optional capabilities and file/function integrations through extension modules.
systems.truth_boundary: Extensions expand behavior but must respect engine compatibility and load policy.
systems.ideal_target: New capabilities plug in without destabilizing core execution.

systems.name: Quality And Compatibility Loop
systems.purpose: Exercises SQL, storage, extension, and compatibility scenarios.
systems.lifecycle: source change -> test cases -> release confidence
systems.truth_boundary: This report does not replace upstream CI or benchmark review.
systems.ideal_target: Engine changes remain behaviorally compatible and measurable.

workflows.name: Parse And Bind
workflows.purpose: Convert SQL into a typed logical representation.

workflows.name: Plan And Optimize
workflows.purpose: Build executable operators while preserving semantics.

workflows.name: Execute Plan
workflows.purpose: Run physical operators and move vectorized data.

workflows.name: Commit Or Return Results
workflows.purpose: Return read results or commit write transactions.

workflows.name: Load Extension
workflows.purpose: Make extension functionality available to query planning and execution.

edges.why: Extensions can make new functions or scan capabilities available to planning.
Execution operators scan data through storage and transaction state.

architecture_patterns.name: Database engine
architecture_patterns.works_for: Embedded databases, query engines, storage engines, and analytical runtimes
architecture_patterns.mapping: Map front end, planner, optimizer, execution, storage, transaction, extension, and test loops.
architecture_patterns.redaction: Public repos can expose source paths; private engines can expose logical layers and interface contracts only.

architecture_patterns.name: Private data strategy
architecture_patterns.works_for: Companies that cannot expose databases or workloads
architecture_patterns.mapping: Use fake SQL examples, logical contracts, benchmark categories, and gate descriptions instead of production queries.
architecture_patterns.redaction: Never publish customer SQL, table names, data files, or workload traces without rights.

walkthroughs.name: One query through the engine
walkthroughs.story: A SQL request is parsed and bound into a logical plan, optimized, converted to physical operators, executed over data chunks, and either returned as a result or committed if it changes storage.

walkthroughs.name: How to audit a private engine without workload data
walkthroughs.story: A reviewer can inspect logical layers, contracts, gates, tests, and fake workloads without seeing private datasets or production SQL.

review_questions: How does SQL move from text to parsed statement, logical plan, optimized plan, physical operators, and result chunks?
Which gates preserve query semantics during binding and optimization?
Where do storage and transaction boundaries constrain execution?
How do extensions expand engine behavior without destabilizing the core?
Which public tests, fuzzers, benchmarks, and release notes would a deeper audit inspect?

rebuild_recipe.goal: Check the DuckDB public repo manifest.
Generate the DuckDB system review report.

known_boundaries: This is a public educational map, not an official DuckDB maintainer audit.
It maps high-level engine layers and public source paths, not every operator or internal invariant.
A real audit should inspect a specific commit, build configuration, tests, fuzzers, benchmarks, and release notes.
Do not use production SQL or private data in public examples.
